headline: ESF's Reserves Hit Record HK$3.75 Billion as Competition Intensifies
dek: Hong Kong's largest international school group has quietly amassed a war chest larger than most local universities, even as government subsidies continue to taper and rivals multiply.
author: marcus-chen
heroAlt: ESF's Reserves Hit Record HK$3.75 Billion as Competition Intensifies

body.text: The English Schools Foundation has ended the 2024–25 academic year with net assets of HK$3.75 billion — a record that surpasses the reserves of both Lingnan University and the Education University of Hong Kong, according to a review of the group's annual reports by the South China Morning Post. The figure is more than three times the HK$1.18 billion the foundation held in 2012–13, representing over a decade of uninterrupted growth.

The reserves have been built primarily from operational surpluses — tuition fee income and capital funds raised through nomination rights — at a time when the government subsidy that once underpinned ESF is being phased out entirely, reaching zero by 2028–29. In 2023–24, government subvention accounted for just five per cent of ESF's operating income, a share that continues to shrink.

The reserve announcement comes weeks after ESF signalled a 4.1 per cent average fee increase for most of its primary and secondary schools in 2026–27 — a smaller rise than the prior year but still adding roughly HK$600 to HK$720 per month for families. At its higher-fee private independent school Discovery College, the proposed increase reaches 4.8 per cent. ESF chief executive Belinda Greer framed the rise as necessary to maintain teaching quality.

Yet the foundation's own annual report acknowledges the pressure bearing down from the other direction: it expects increased competition for students and has said it will modernise its marketing strategies to attract the children of the talent and investors settling in the city under various government attraction schemes. That is a pointed admission from an organisation that has historically relied on its scale and established reputation to fill places across its 22 schools and more than 18,000 enrolments.

For operators of independent British schools in Hong Kong — Harrow, Kellett, Shrewsbury and the growing cohort of new entrants such as NLCS Hong Kong — the ESF figures carry two messages. The first is that the group is not financially fragile, despite subsidy cuts; the second is that it is girding itself for a more competitive market. ESF is also opening three new kindergarten locations in 2026, a sign that it is betting on enrolment at the early-years level to anchor families through secondary.

The reserve surplus also invites a question that legislators have raised before: whether a school group of this size, still in receipt of public subsidy, should be accumulating a balance sheet that rivals tertiary institutions. ESF has not commented publicly on that framing, but as the subsidy line approaches zero and the competitive landscape widens, the scrutiny is unlikely to diminish.
